mini_headings_covered: What are the tropical rain forests?

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: A forest fire destroys most of the trees in a woodland area, but the soil remains intact. Why can this ecosystem recover more quickly than a newly formed volcanic island?

options.A: Because fire adds nutrients to the soil
options.B: Because the soil already contains seeds and organic matter
options.C: Because fire kills all organisms instantly
options.D: Because volcanic islands have more rainfall

questions.hint: Think about what is already present in the soil after a fire versus on new volcanic rock.
questions.explanation: Secondary succession after fire occurs faster because soil and some organisms remain. Option A is partially true but not the primary reason for faster recovery compared to primary succession on bare rock.

explanations_tiered.definition: An ecosystem is a self-supporting unit containing a community of organisms and their non-living environment.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Ecosystems recycle nutrients internally and depend on sunlight for energy input.
explanations_tiered.beginner: An ecosystem is like a self-contained neighborhood where plants, animals, and the environment all work together.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: In a woodland ecosystem, plants absorb light and rainwater; animals feed on plants and each other; decomposers return nutrients to soil.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Ecosystems are characterized by energy flow (from sun to producers to consumers) and nutrient cycling (carbon, nitrogen, phosphorus).
reasoning_steps: Identify that soil contains seeds and organic matter after fire.
Recognize that volcanic rock has no soil initially.
Conclude that existing soil speeds recovery.
misconceptions: Fire does not always destroy all organic matter; soil often remains viable.
Volcanic islands lack soil entirely, so succession starts from bare rock (primary).
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Secondary succession faster because soil remains.

glossary_entry.definition: A biological community of interacting organisms and their physical environment.

flashcard.front: Why does secondary succession occur faster than primary succession?
flashcard.back: Secondary succession starts with soil containing seeds and organic matter; primary succession starts on bare rock with no soil.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: What remains after a forest fire that speeds up ecosystem recovery?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: Soil containing seeds and organic matter remains, allowing faster secondary succession compared to primary succession on bare rock.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: A community of organisms and their physical environment interacting as a system.
publishing_metadata.revision_note: Existing soil = faster recovery after disturbance.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Compare secondary succession (fire) vs. primary succession (volcanic island).
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Explain the terrestrial and aquatic ecosystems
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. Soil contains seeds and organic matter, enabling faster recovery than bare volcanic rock.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.structural_anchor: What are the tropical rain forests?
questions.question: Which of the following is a characteristic of tropical rain forests?

options.A: Annual rainfall of 130 to 200 centimeters and year-round warm temperatures
options.B: Less than 10 centimeters of rain per year and extreme temperature fluctuations
options.C: Cold winters with snow and coniferous trees as the dominant vegetation
options.D: Seasonal rainfall with grasses as the dominant plant type

questions.hint: Tropical rain forests are known for high rainfall and constant warmth.
questions.explanation: Tropical rain forests receive 130-200 cm of rain annually with average temperatures around 25°C. Option B describes deserts, not tropical rain forests.

source_trace.structural_element: What are the tropical rain forests?
source_trace.content_anchor: Rain that falls throughout the year sums to an annual total of 130 to 200 centimetres. Regular rains, combined with an average temperature of 25°C

explanations_tiered.definition: Tropical rain forests are terrestrial biomes characterized by high annual rainfall (130-200 cm) and consistently warm temperatures around 25°C.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: These forests have vertically layered vegetation, high biodiversity, and year-round photosynthesis due to constant day length.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Rain forests get lots of rain every year and are always warm — like a hot, wet jungle.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Tropical rain forests receive 130-200 cm of rainfall annually and maintain temperatures near 25°C, supporting the highest biodiversity of any terrestrial biome.
explanations_tiered.advanced: The constant warm temperature and high humidity in tropical rain forests allow net primary productivity to remain high throughout the year without seasonal dormancy.
reasoning_steps: Recall that tropical rain forests have high rainfall.
Recall that they are warm year-round.
Match these to the option with both high rain and warm temps.
misconceptions: Not all forests with rain are tropical; temperate rain forests exist but have cooler temperatures.
Tropical rain forests are not all at the equator; they occur between 10°N and 10°S latitude.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Tropical rain forest: 130-200 cm rain, 25°C, high biodiversity.

glossary_entry.definition: A terrestrial biome with high rainfall (130-200 cm/year), warm temperatures year-round, and extremely high biodiversity.

flashcard.front: What are the two main climate characteristics of tropical rain forests?
flashcard.back: High annual rainfall (130-200 cm) and consistently warm temperatures (around 25°C).

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Which biome has the highest biodiversity and receives 130-200 cm of rain per year?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: Tropical rain forests have the highest biodiversity and receive 130-200 cm of annual rainfall with warm temperatures year-round.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: A warm, wet biome with extremely high species diversity.
publishing_metadata.revision_note: Rain forests: high rain, warm, high biodiversity.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Contrast tropical rain forests with temperate forests (cold winters, deciduous trees).
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer A. Tropical rain forests have 130-200 cm rain and warm temperatures year-round.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: How do succulent plants such as cacti adapt to survive in desert conditions?

options.A: By growing tall to reach sunlight above other plants
options.B: By storing water in their tissues and reducing leaf surface area
options.C: By dropping all their leaves during the dry season
options.D: By developing deep taproots that reach the water table

questions.hint: Desert plants need to conserve water in a dry environment.
questions.explanation: Succulents store water in their tissues and have reduced leaf area (spines instead of leaves) to minimize water loss. Option D describes phreatophytes, not all succulents.

source_trace.content_anchor: Desert plant adaptations include tolerance of heat and desiccation, water storage, and reduced leaf surface area

explanations_tiered.definition: Deserts are biomes with very low rainfall (less than 10 cm per year) and extreme temperature fluctuations between day and night.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Desert plants have evolved water-storage tissues, reduced leaves, and deep roots to survive arid conditions.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Cacti store water in their thick stems like a water bottle and have spines instead of leaves to save water.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Succulent plants store water in parenchyma tissue and reduce transpiration by having spines or small waxy leaves.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Crassulacean acid metabolism (CAM) photosynthesis allows succulents to open stomata at night, reducing water loss by up to 95% compared to C3 plants.
reasoning_steps: Identify that deserts have very little water.
Consider how plants can store water and reduce water loss.
Select the option that describes water storage and reduced leaf area.
misconceptions: Not all desert plants are succulents; some are drought-deciduous shrubs.
Cacti spines are modified leaves, not just for defense — they also reduce air flow and water loss.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Desert adaptations: store water + reduce leaf area.

glossary_entry.definition: A plant adapted to arid conditions by storing water in fleshy tissues such as stems or leaves.

flashcard.front: How do cacti survive in deserts with very little water?
flashcard.back: Cacti store water in their thick stems and have spines instead of leaves to reduce water loss through transpiration.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: What are two adaptations of succulent plants in deserts?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: Water storage in fleshy tissues and reduced leaf surface area (spines) to minimize transpiration.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: A plant adapted to dry conditions by storing water in its tissues.
publishing_metadata.revision_note: Succulents: store water, spines reduce water loss.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Connect adaptation to function: spines reduce surface area for transpiration.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Mention the fauna and flora of each biome
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. Succulents store water and reduce leaf area to survive desert dryness.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Which biome is characterized by scattered trees, seasonal rainfall of 30-50 cm per year, and large herds of grazing herbivores?
questions.hint: Think of African wildlife such as zebras, giraffes, and wildebeests.
questions.explanation: Savannas have scattered trees, 30-50 cm annual rainfall with a long dry season, and support large herbivores like zebras and wildebeests. Option A describes prairies with fewer trees.

source_trace.content_anchor: precipitation averages 30-50 cm per year ... The biome is characterized by scattered trees ... Herbivores include giraffes, zebras, elephants

explanations_tiered.definition: Savanna is a tropical grassland biome with scattered trees, seasonal rainfall (30-50 cm/year), and a distinct dry season lasting up to nine months.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Savannas support large herbivores such as zebras, giraffes, and elephants, along with predators like lions and hyenas.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Savannas are warm grasslands with a few trees here and there, like the African plains where lions and zebras live.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Savannas experience a long dry season during which fires are common, and grasses are adapted to both fire and grazing.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Fire ecology in savannas maintains the balance between grasses and trees; fire frequency prevents tree encroachment while promoting grass regrowth.
reasoning_steps: Note the key features: scattered trees, 30-50 cm rain, large herbivores.
Recall that savannas match this description.
Eliminate temperate grasslands (fewer trees, colder winters) and tundra (very cold).
misconceptions: Savannas are not deserts; they receive more rainfall than deserts (<10 cm).
Savannas are not tropical rain forests; they have a long dry season and fewer trees.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Savanna: scattered trees, 30-50 cm rain, large herbivores.

glossary_entry.definition: A tropical grassland biome with scattered trees, seasonal rainfall, and large herds of grazing mammals.

flashcard.front: Which biome has scattered trees, 30-50 cm of rain per year, and zebras?
flashcard.back: Savanna. It has seasonal rainfall, scattered trees, and supports large herbivores like zebras and wildebeests.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: What are the main characteristics of the savanna biome?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: Savannas have scattered trees, seasonal rainfall (30-50 cm/year), and large herds of grazing herbivores such as zebras and giraffes.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: A tropical grassland with scattered trees and seasonal rainfall.
publishing_metadata.revision_note: Savanna: scattered trees, seasonal rain, large herbivores.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Compare savanna to temperate grassland (prairie) — savanna has scattered trees, prairie has almost none.
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. Savannas have scattered trees, 30-50 cm rain, and large herbivores like zebras.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: What is permafrost and how does it affect plant growth in the tundra?

options.A: A frozen layer of soil that restricts root growth
options.B: A thick layer of ice that melts completely each summer
options.C: A type of rock that prevents water drainage
options.D: A warm soil layer that promotes deep root systems

questions.explanation: Permafrost is a permanently frozen layer of soil that restricts root growth and prevents deep rooting in tundra plants. Option B is incorrect because permafrost does not melt completely each summer.

source_trace.content_anchor: A permanently frozen layer of soil called permafrost restricts the growth of plant roots

explanations_tiered.definition: Permafrost is a permanently frozen layer of soil beneath the surface in cold regions such as the tundra.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Permafrost restricts root depth, prevents tree growth, and influences water drainage and carbon storage.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Permafrost is ground that stays frozen all year, like a hard layer under the soil that roots cannot grow through.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Permafrost limits tundra plants to shallow root systems because the frozen layer prevents deep root penetration.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Permafrost thaw due to climate change releases methane and carbon dioxide from previously frozen organic matter, creating a positive feedback loop.
reasoning_steps: Recall definition of permafrost: permanently frozen.
Consider how frozen soil affects roots: roots cannot grow through ice.
Select the option describing restricted root growth.
misconceptions: Permafrost does not melt completely in summer; only the active layer above it thaws.
Permafrost is not ice alone; it is soil, rock, or sediment held together by ice.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Permafrost = permanently frozen soil, restricts roots.

glossary_entry.definition: A layer of soil or rock that remains frozen for at least two consecutive years.

flashcard.front: What is permafrost and how does it affect tundra plants?
flashcard.back: Permafrost is permanently frozen soil that restricts root growth, limiting plants to shallow root systems.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Why do tundra plants have shallow root systems?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: Permafrost, a permanently frozen layer of soil, restricts root growth and prevents deep root penetration.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Permanently frozen ground that restricts plant root growth.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Explain that only the active layer thaws in summer; permafrost remains frozen.
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer A. Permafrost is permanently frozen soil that restricts root growth in the tundra.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Why do lakes sometimes experience fish kills after an algal bloom?

options.A: Algae release toxic chemicals that kill fish directly
options.B: Algal blooms block sunlight, preventing photosynthesis
options.C: Decomposition of dead algae depletes dissolved oxygen
options.D: Algae consume all the available carbon dioxide

questions.hint: Think about what happens to oxygen when large amounts of organic matter decompose.
questions.explanation: When algae die, decomposers (bacteria) break them down using oxygen, leading to oxygen depletion and fish kills. Option B is partially true but oxygen depletion is the direct cause of fish death.

source_trace.content_anchor: nutrient enrichment, which can produce large numbers of algae (an algal 'bloom') oxygen depletion, and fish kills

explanations_tiered.definition: Eutrophication is the nutrient enrichment of water bodies, leading to algal blooms, oxygen depletion, and fish kills.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Decomposers consume dissolved oxygen when breaking down dead algae, creating hypoxic conditions that kill fish.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Too many algae grow, then they die and rot. Rotting uses up oxygen in the water, so fish suffocate.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Algal blooms block light, but the primary cause of fish kills is bacterial decomposition of dead algae depleting dissolved oxygen.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Hypoxia occurs when biochemical oxygen demand (BOD) exceeds oxygen replenishment rate, creating dead zones as seen in the Gulf of Mexico.
reasoning_steps: Algal bloom occurs from excess nutrients.
Algae die and sink.
Decomposers break down algae using oxygen.
Oxygen depletion kills fish.
misconceptions: Algae do not directly kill fish in most cases; oxygen depletion kills fish.
Algal blooms themselves produce oxygen during the day; the problem occurs after they die.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Algal bloom → decomposition → oxygen depletion → fish kill.

glossary_entry.definition: A rapid increase in algae population due to nutrient enrichment, often leading to oxygen depletion when algae die.

flashcard.front: Why do fish die after an algal bloom in a lake?
flashcard.back: When algae die, decomposers break them down and consume dissolved oxygen, depleting oxygen levels and suffocating fish.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: What is the chain of events from algal bloom to fish kill?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: Algal bloom → algae die → decomposers use oxygen → oxygen depletion → fish suffocate and die.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Rapid growth of algae caused by nutrient pollution.
publishing_metadata.revision_note: Bloom → die → decompose → oxygen low → fish die.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Connect to eutrophication from fertilizer runoff in agriculture.
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer C. Bacterial decomposition of dead algae consumes dissolved oxygen, leading to fish kills.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: A caterpillar eats the leaves of a plant, damaging the plant but not killing it. This interaction is an example of:
questions.explanation: Herbivory is an exploitative interaction where an herbivore eats parts of a plant or alga, harming it but usually not killing it. Option A (predation) typically involves killing the prey.

source_trace.content_anchor: Herbivory is an exploitative interaction in which an organism—an herbivore—eats parts of a plant or alga, thereby harming it but usually not killing it

explanations_tiered.definition: Herbivory is an interaction where an animal (herbivore) consumes plant or algal tissue, harming the plant but typically not killing it.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Herbivores include insects, grazing mammals, and some birds; they have specialized teeth or digestive systems for processing vegetation.
explanations_tiered.beginner: A caterpillar eating a leaf is herbivory — the plant is hurt but usually survives.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Herbivory differs from predation in that the herbivore usually does not kill the plant, though repeated damage can reduce plant fitness.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Plants have evolved chemical and physical defenses against herbivory, including tannins, spines, and volatile organic compounds that attract predators of herbivores.
reasoning_steps: Identify what the caterpillar eats: plant leaves.
Recall that animals eating plants are herbivores.
Match to term 'herbivory'.
misconceptions: Herbivory is not mutualism; the plant is harmed (-).
Not all herbivores are mammals; insects are the most numerous herbivores.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Herbivory (+/-): animal eats plant, plant harmed.

glossary_entry.definition: The consumption of plant or algal tissues by an animal (herbivore), which harms the plant but usually does not kill it.

flashcard.front: What is the difference between herbivory and predation?
flashcard.back: Herbivory involves eating plants (usually not killing them); predation involves killing and eating animals.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: What term describes an animal eating plant tissue without necessarily killing the plant?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: Herbivory. Herbivores eat plants or algae, harming them but usually not killing them.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: The act of eating plant or algal tissues.
publishing_metadata.revision_note: Herbivory: plant eaten, plant harmed but not killed.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Compare herbivory (+/-) to mutualism where both benefit.
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. The caterpillar is an herbivore eating plant tissue; this is herbivory.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Cattle egrets follow grazing bison and eat insects that are flushed out of the grass. The bison are not affected by the birds. This relationship is classified as:
questions.hint: One species benefits; the other is neither helped nor harmed.
questions.explanation: Commensalism is an interaction that benefits one species but does not help or harm the other. The egrets benefit from easier feeding; the bison are unaffected.

source_trace.content_anchor: Commensalism is interaction that neither benefits the individuals of one of the interacting species but neither harms nor helps the individuals of the other species

explanations_tiered.definition: Commensalism is an ecological interaction where one species benefits and the other is neither helped nor harmed (+/0).
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Examples include barnacles on whales, birds nesting in trees, and egrets following grazing animals.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Commensalism is like getting a free ride without helping or hurting the other person.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: In commensalism, the commensal benefits (food, shelter, transport) while the host remains unaffected.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Commensal relationships can evolve into mutualism if the host later derives benefit, as seen in some cleaning symbioses.
reasoning_steps: Identify who benefits: egrets benefit (food).
Identify effect on bison: no effect (neither helped nor harmed).
Match to commensalism (+/0).
misconceptions: Commensalism is not parasitism; the host is not harmed.
Commensal relationships may actually have hidden costs; strict commensalism is rare in nature.

glossary_entry.definition: An interaction in which one species benefits and the other is neither helped nor harmed.

flashcard.front: What distinguishes commensalism from mutualism and parasitism?
flashcard.flashcard_back: Commensalism benefits one species (+), the other is unaffected (0). Mutualism benefits both (+/+); parasitism benefits one and harms the other (+/-).
flashcard.glossary_definition: A relationship where one organism benefits and the other is unaffected.
flashcard.teacher_note: Use cattle egret example: birds get food, bison unaffected.
flashcard.solution_manual_note: Answer B. Egrets benefit; bison are unaffected → commensalism.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Which of the following scenarios best illustrates an evolutionary adaptation resulting from predator-prey interactions?

options.A: A cactus storing water in its stem to survive drought
options.B: A gazelle developing faster running speed over generations
options.C: A tree growing taller to reach sunlight in a forest
options.D: A fungus breaking down dead leaves for nutrients

questions.hint: Consider which adaptation helps an organism avoid being eaten.
questions.explanation: Predator-prey interactions drive natural selection for escape adaptations like speed, camouflage, or warning coloration. Option B shows a prey adaptation (speed) evolved to escape predators.

source_trace.content_anchor: Because eating and avoiding being eaten are prerequisites to reproductive success, the adaptations of both predators and prey tend to be refined through natural selection

explanations_tiered.definition: Predation is an interaction where one organism (predator) kills and eats another organism (prey).
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Predator-prey coevolution leads to adaptations such as speed, camouflage, toxins, and warning coloration.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Gazelles that run fast are less likely to be caught by lions, so over time gazelles get faster.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Natural selection favors prey that can escape predators and predators that can catch prey, leading to an evolutionary arms race.
explanations_tiered.advanced: The Lotka-Volterra model \(\frac{dN}{dt} = rN - aNP\) and \(\frac{dP}{dt} = bNP - mP\) describes cyclic predator-prey population dynamics.
reasoning_steps: Identify that the question asks for adaptation from predator-prey interaction.
Recognize that speed in gazelles helps escape predators.
Eliminate options related to abiotic factors (water, light) or decomposition.
misconceptions: Not all fast animals evolved speed due to predators; some evolved for hunting or migration.
Predator-prey adaptations are coevolutionary: both sides evolve in response to each other.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Predator-prey arms race → adaptations like speed.

glossary_entry.definition: An interaction in which one organism (predator) kills and eats another organism (prey).

flashcard.front: How does predator-prey interaction drive evolution?
flashcard.flashcard_back: Predators evolve better hunting adaptations; prey evolve better escape adaptations (speed, camouflage) through natural selection.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: What type of adaptation results from predator-prey coevolution?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: Prey evolve speed, camouflage, or toxins; predators evolve better senses, speed, or hunting strategies.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: An interaction where one organism kills and eats another.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Classic example: cheetah speed vs. gazelle speed — both have increased over evolutionary time.
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. Faster running speed in gazelles is an adaptation to escape predators, driven by predator-prey interactions.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: In a mycorrhizal association, fungi transfer phosphorus to plant roots while plants transfer carbohydrates to the fungi. This relationship is best described as:

options.A: Commensalism because only the plant benefits
options.B: Parasitism because the fungus takes nutrients from the plant
options.C: Mutualism because both organisms benefit from the exchange
options.D: Competition because both require the same nutrients

questions.hint: Both organisms gain something valuable from the interaction.
questions.explanation: Mutualism benefits both species: the fungus receives carbohydrates, and the plant receives phosphorus and other nutrients. Option B incorrectly identifies a mutualism as parasitism.

source_trace.content_anchor: Mutualism is interaction that benefits individuals of both of the interacting species ... mycorrhizae

explanations_tiered.definition: Mutualism is an ecological interaction where both participating species derive fitness benefits.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Mycorrhizae are mutualistic associations between fungi and plant roots; fungi enhance nutrient uptake, plants provide carbohydrates.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Mycorrhizae are like a trade: the plant gives the fungus sugar, and the fungus gives the plant minerals.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: In mycorrhizal mutualism, the plant transfers carbohydrates to the fungus, and the fungus transfers limiting nutrients like phosphorus to the plant.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Mycorrhizal networks can connect multiple plants, enabling nutrient transfer between individuals and even between different plant species.
reasoning_steps: Identify what the plant gains: phosphorus.
Identify what the fungus gains: carbohydrates.
Both benefit → mutualism.
misconceptions: Mycorrhizae are not parasitic; both partners incur costs but net benefit is positive.
Not all fungi associated with plant roots are mutualists; some are pathogenic.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Mutualism: both benefit (plant gets P, fungus gets sugar).

glossary_entry.definition: A mutualistic association between a fungus and plant roots, where the fungus enhances nutrient uptake and the plant provides carbohydrates.

flashcard.front: What is exchanged in a mycorrhizal mutualism?
flashcard.flashcard_back: Fungus transfers phosphorus and other nutrients to the plant; plant transfers carbohydrates to the fungus.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: What type of relationship exists between mycorrhizal fungi and plant roots?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: Mutualism: fungi receive carbohydrates from plants; plants receive phosphorus and other nutrients from fungi.
publishing_metadata.revision_note: Mycorrhizae: mutualism (plant gets P, fungus gets sugar).
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Emphasize that mycorrhizae are essential for most terrestrial plants.
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer C. Both species benefit (plant gets nutrients, fungus gets carbohydrates) → mutualism.
